prerequisites: A Slack workspace you can authenticate against, ideally a developer sandbox to avoid disrupting a production workspace.
A Slack app created on the Slack App Management platform (api.slack.com/apps), which is the source of all tokens and OAuth credentials.
Permission to install the app to the workspace and to grant the OAuth scopes the app requests.
A start_date marking the earliest point from which to pull messages, plus a lookback_window and the join_channels setting, all of which are required config fields.
If you set include_private_channels, the authenticating token must already have access to those private channels; private conversations are only visible to members.

authMethods.name: OAuth access token (bot or user token)
authMethods.summary: Maps to the credentials.access_token secret field. Installing a Slack app to a workspace via OAuth produces an access token: a Bot User OAuth Token (prefixed xoxb-) that acts as the app, or a User OAuth Token (prefixed xoxp-) that acts as the authorizing user with the same workspace visibility that user has. Configure the bot/user scopes the connector needs on the app's OAuth & Permissions page, install the app, then supply the resulting token. Treat the token like a password.

authMethods.name: API token
authMethods.summary: Maps to the credentials.api_token secret field. Supply a Slack API token directly as the credential instead of running the full OAuth installation handoff. The token still originates from a Slack app and carries whatever scopes were granted to it; provide it as the credentials.api_token value.

authMethods.name: OAuth client secret
authMethods.summary: Maps to the credentials.client_secret secret field. The client secret is one half of an app's OAuth credentials (paired with the client ID), both found on the app's App Management settings. It is used together with the temporary authorization code in the oauth.v2.access exchange to obtain an access token, proving the request comes from your app. Keep it confidential.

setupSteps.title: Create a Slack app
setupSteps.body: Go to the Slack App Management platform at api.slack.com/apps and create a new app (you can create it from scratch or from a manifest). Select the workspace you want to develop against. The app is the origin of every token and OAuth credential the connector can use.

setupSteps.title: Add OAuth scopes and install the app
setupSteps.body: On the app's OAuth & Permissions page, add the bot and/or user scopes the connector requires, then install the app to your workspace. Installation runs the OAuth flow and produces a Bot User OAuth Token (xoxb-) or User OAuth Token (xoxp-). Supply that value as credentials.access_token. To authenticate with a direct token instead, provide it as credentials.api_token.

setupSteps.title: Capture OAuth client credentials (if exchanging codes yourself)
setupSteps.body: If you complete the OAuth handoff rather than pasting an existing token, copy the app's Client ID and Client Secret from its App Management settings. The client secret is used with the temporary authorization code in the oauth.v2.access call to obtain an access token. Provide the secret as credentials.client_secret and keep it confidential.

setupSteps.title: Configure ingestion settings
setupSteps.body: Set the required fields: start_date (earliest point to pull from), lookback_window, and join_channels. Optionally tune channel_filter, channel_messages_window_size, include_private_channels, include_archived_channels, threads_ignore_no_replies, and num_workers. Note that include_private_channels only surfaces private channels the authenticating token can already see.

sources.title: Slack quickstart (create app, scopes, install, get bot token)
